title: Leading bipartisan lawmakers on how to achieve US energy ‘dominance’
gist: US lawmakers describe the bipartisan DOMINANCE Act (passed the House, headed to the Senate) and the Senate's Energy Security Pacts Act as responses to China's critical-mineral export restrictions and the Strait of Hormuz crisis. The core diagnosis: the US has 'plenty' of rare earths but lacks refining/processing capacity (billions of dollars and ~29-year permitting timelines), and policy aims to give industry and allies durable, cross-administration certainty.

insights.claim: Rep. Ami Bera argues the US has 'plenty' of rare earth elements and that the real constraint is refining/processing capacity, requiring billions of dollars of investment, with the gap caused by Beijing subsidizing the industry and squeezing out US firms.
insights.speaker: srothbardt
insights.so_what: The investable bottleneck is midstream processing/refining, not mining—where US policy support and capital will concentrate.
insights.quote: it’s the refining capacity that is a real challenge.

insights.claim: It takes on average twenty-nine years to bring a mine online in the US, most of that spent on permitting, per a US mining executive operating in Peru cited by Rep. Young Kim.
insights.speaker: srothbardt
insights.so_what: Permitting timelines make near-term US mine supply implausible—reinforces the refining/recycling and allied-sourcing thesis over greenfield mining.
insights.quote: It takes on average twenty-nine years to bring a mine online (most of that time being spent on permitting).

insights.claim: The House passed the bipartisan DOMINANCE Act, which focuses on critical minerals and would create a Bureau of Energy Security and Diplomacy within the State Department as a single command center coordinating US energy and critical-mineral strategy.
insights.speaker: srothbardt
insights.so_what: A concrete legislative catalyst for US critical-mineral policy—track Senate passage as a sector tailwind.
insights.quote: Our allies need one command center with one strategy, so we can all work together, and this is creating that

insights.claim: The Senate is working on the Energy Security Pacts Act (Coons/Ricketts) to establish tools to coordinate US foreign investments in partner countries' energy systems.
insights.speaker: srothbardt
insights.so_what: Signals US capital/coordination flowing into allied energy projects—relevant for allied energy and minerals developers.
insights.locator: The DOMINANCE Act, which now heads to the Senate

insights.claim: Sen. Chris Coons frames the legislative aim as building structures that 'transcend any particular administration' to give a durable energy trajectory, because critical-mineral investments are typically high cost, high risk, and long term.
insights.speaker: srothbardt
insights.so_what: Policy durability across elections is the variable that unlocks long-horizon private capital into minerals—watch whether it survives politically.
insights.quote: build structures that transcend any particular administration

insights.claim: Lawmakers cite China's critical-mineral export restrictions and Iran's hold on the Strait of Hormuz as the proximate triggers, while Bera names the pandemic supply-chain disruption as the original 'wake-up call' revealing total dependence on China.
insights.speaker: srothbardt
insights.so_what: Frames the political durability of de-risking from China—a multi-year structural demand driver for non-China mineral supply chains.
insights.quote: We realized our total dependence on a single nation, in this case, China.
insights.locator: But for US Representative Ami Bera
